title: The Map Method 🗺️
description: Transform arrays beautifully with a single perfect line of code.

slides.title: The Transformation Problem 🔄
slides.content:
Imagine you have an Array of numbers: `[1, 2, 3]`.

You want to elegantly create a brand new Array where every single number is magically flawlessly doubled: `[2, 4, 6]`.

Using a standard `.each` loop is incredibly clunky because you have to manually build an empty array and physically `.push` into it.

slides.title: Enter `.map` 🗺️
slides.content:
The Enumerable module provides `.map` (sometimes called `.collect`).

`.map` is identical to `.each`, but with one massive superpower: it explicitly beautifully collects whatever value the Block implicitly returns, and uses it to automatically construct a brand new transformed Array for you!

slides.title: Map in Action
slides.content: Run this! Notice how flawlessly `.map` takes the returning result of `num * 2` and secretly gracefully builds a brand new array filled with those results.
slides.codeSnippet:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4]

# Map automatically catches the result of the block
doubled = numbers.map do |num|
  num * 2
end

# We now have two completely separate arrays
puts "Original: #{numbers}"
puts "Doubled: #{doubled}"

quizData.question: What is the extreme functional difference between `.each` and `.map`?

options.text: `.each` is for Hashes, `.map` is for Arrays.
`.each` just blindly loops. `.map` loops, captures the return value of the block, and cleanly builds a brand new transformed array from those values.

quizData.explanation: If you want to gracefully fundamentally TRANSFORM an array of data into a new array, you always use `.map`.

slides.title: The Exclamation Mark Map! ‼️
slides.content:
By default, `.map` playfully returns a brand new Array and safely leaves the original Array perfectly untouched.

If you violently want to permanently mutate and destroy the original Array, neatly overwriting it immediately with the new data, you use the 'bang' version: `.map!`

slides.title: Map Bang
slides.content: Run this! Because we used the exclamation mark `!`, the original data is violently permanently altered in memory.
slides.codeSnippet:
names = ["alice", "bob"]

# The exclamation mark permanently mutates the original!
names.map! do |name|
  name.capitalize
end

puts names

quizData.question: What does appending an exclamation mark (the 'bang') typically mean in Ruby method names, like `.map!` or `.sort!`?

options.text: It means the method violently permanently mutates (destroys) the original object data instead of generating a safe copy.

quizData.explanation: The bang `!` universally signals 'Warning! I am about to physically alter the raw memory of this variable definitively!'
